title: Create Even More Schema
content_md:
# Create Even More Schema

- Code: 975cf5d5
- Source: https://www.skool.com/ai-seo-mastery-pro/classroom/63a81f62?md=975cf5d58b364063acae91d4b7035b19
- Video: https://www.loom.com/share/eef2c1a4e0bc42f397bf592be35d53c6?sid=32592a70-a97d-44f5-bb09-83fb06773567?hide_owner=true&hide_share=true&hide_title=true&hideEmbedTopBar=true
- Duration: 5m 46s
- Resources:
  - [Schema Markup Generator [Tool]](https://technicalseo.com/tools/schema-markup-generator/)
  - [Schema Validator [Tool]](https://validator.schema.org/)

## Description

_(no description)_

transcript.text: Let's talk about schema.
I hopefully you're ready to hear me say schema many, many times.
OK, now, obviously, if you don't know this,
schema does not affect how users see your site.
Users will never see schema.
It's code embedded in your site.
It is designed to help Google enhance some of the search engine
results page features.
Additionally, ChatGPT heavily relies on schema
to interpret your business legitimacy.
It's important to note, of course,
that Google develops schema, not ChatGPT.
Schema has existed for a long time,
and it was really used by Google.
What we think about schema, it allows you, using code,
to basically tell AI directly and Google directly.
It's the only way that you can directly connect and communicate
with AI and Google that are crawling your website.
You tell them who you are, what you offer, where you serve,
when you're open, why people trust you.
So the example that I like to think about when
I think about schema and what it is, is moving boxes.
So if you're moving from one place to another place,
you need to pack all of your worldly possessions into boxes,
move them into the new place, and then unpack them.
Schema is the label on those moving boxes.
You don't need to label the moving boxes.
You could move them all over to the new place,
open the moving boxes, and then rummage around
and try to figure out what it is.
But I can guarantee to you that moving day will
be so much easier if you label the moving boxes,
so much easier to unpack, and know where they go.
That is schema.
So the absolute minimum schema that you should be using,
and this is for Google, right?
For Google and for ChatGPT is local business.
Local business only goes on the GVP landing page.
Organization, postal address, and opening hours, right?
Organization can go everywhere.
And the other two also only on the GVP landing page.
So this is the absolute minimum schema you should,
I'm trying to get, there you go.
You should have the schema anyway
if you are trying to rank on Google.
There's more schema that we should put in
to help ChatGPT to help AI.
We should have service schema.
That lists on each service you offer with descriptions.
Yes, these are also on your GVP,
but we'll talk about the language
that you should use in these descriptions
for your AI schema.
You should have product schema if you have products.
You should FAQ schema.
That is massively helpful to ChatGPT
to match your content to user questions.
Because remember, the ways ChatGPT works
is it generates answers based on user questions.
This is much more powerful than simply asking people
also ask questions on Google
as the entire search on ChatGPT
is going to be conversational with questions.
Okay, review schema.
That doesn't really work for Google anymore,
but you can still use it for ChatGPT
to inject the real testimonials into the data.
Aggregate rating, that's similar to the review.
We have the geo-coordinates, the service area, okay?
Now, you can even go more advanced on this.
One thing that we really like doing is nesting schema.
So you have the local business schema
and inside that schema you can add service schema
and talk about the services that you offer
inside the local business schema.
If you don't know how to code JSON,
no problem, ChatGPT does.
It's a great way to actually get all of this written for you.
Just tell ChatGPT that you want to use nested schema.
It'll take care of coding it for you.
Okay, now, the FAQ content,
you want to mirror the prompts
that users are typing into ChatGPT, right?
So do you offer emergency services?
How much does service cost?
Use the language that users are typing into ChatGPT.
I know I said exact match language
isn't nearly as valuable for ChatGPT
as it was for Google,
but if you can mirror users language
that is going to make it much easier for ChatGPT
to match up the semantically relevant answers, okay?
Talked about the actual view quotes
within the review schema and yeah,
mimicking writing language.
So this is an example of bad schema.
So this is service schema
and the service is emergency plumbing
and the description we offer professional
emergency plumbing services 24 hours a day,
seven days a week.
No one talks like that, right?
That's not how somebody's going to ask ChatGPT
to find an emergency plumber near them.
Instead, we could write need help fast,
we fix burst pipes, leaks and clogs 24-7 within the hour.
Much more powerful at showing ChatGPT exactly what you do
and mimicking the language that somebody would ask ChatGPT
in order to get ChatGPT,
more likely to recommend you
because you've used semantically similar language
in your schema.
I know I am beating on this,
but it is really that important.
Yeah, use language that matches user intent,
natural phrases, compositional speech
when you are doing the schema.
And here are some tools.
Honestly, usually I'm just using ChatGPT
to write the schema.
There's a tool here that'll generate it for you,
but it's not going to be able to generate
as much of the advanced stuff as ChatGPT will.
So you could start here with something simple
like local business schema or service schema
and then ask ChatGPT to make it the nested form
or just go straight to ChatGPT, tell it what you want.
You can even ask ChatGPT what schema you should do
and then ask it to generate it for you.
Always test your schema.
This web address here is going to validate it.
If there are any errors, right?
If there are any errors or any warnings,
give ChatGPT that error or warning and tell it to fix it.

summary: The lesson emphasizes the importance of using schema markup to enhance search engine results and improve AI interpretation. It covers various types of schema such as local business, service, product, FAQ, and review schemas, with a focus on using natural language that matches user intent.
key_points: mirror user prompts in FAQ content
